item.content:encoded: Ah, 2399. The first article I ever read on-site. Blew my simple Series I knowing brain then, still love it now.

item.title: Re: [DATA EXPUNGED]
item.content:encoded:
Woah, series 2000 was six years ago already?

I think I stepped into some temporal anomaly myself and everything is speeding up.

item.title: Re: [DATA EXPUNGED]
item.content:encoded: This article was written nearly six years ago - you just have to take it for what it is. Signs of a different time.

item.title: [DATA EXPUNGED]
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea potential, but not the execution.

After the events of ██/██/██, it was decided that necessary force would be authorized to destroy or incapacitate SCP-2399. Using Foundation resources, as well as resources from 45 nations (notably, █████ ███████, ███████, █████ ██████, ██████, ███████ ██████, ██████'█ ████████ ██ █████, and ██████████ ██████'█ ████████ ██ █████), a platform of ██ warheads bearing ███ Mt payloads and ██ warheads bearing EMP detonators was launched and placed in orbit around Europa. On ██/██/██ at ████ hours with orders from 15 heads of state and O5█, O5█, O5█, O5█, and O5█, the entire payload of Project Gigas was launched towards SCP-2399.

Paragraphs like this look like something out of a joke scp or a parody.

item.title: A Plot Hole of the Non-Worm Type
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I just listened to a dramatized version of SCP-2399, and I really enjoyed it!

There's one thing that gets me though.

You write that SCP 2399 originated from the Triangulum Galaxy (roughly 2.73 million light years away). It crashed into Jupiter in 1665. According to Addendum 2399-Comm-Log, the alien base back in the Triangulum Galaxy sent a message to the object (repair damages), which we intercepted in 1971.

Hol' up.

Even if the alien object immediately sent a message back to base reporting the damage, that message would have taken approximately 2,730,000 years to get there, and the response ("Unit is damaged: Repair") would have taken another 2,730,000 years to get back to the solar system. In other words, that message would not even be around for us to intercept until later than 5,460,000 AD.

One initial thought I had was that maybe the aliens were using some sort of worm hole to communicate with each other directly. However, this can't work, because then humanity's efforts to jam their communications wouldn't have worked as stated in the SCP article. So worm holes can't fix this plot hole.

The best solution I can think of is to change the stated source of the alien warship from the Triangulum Galaxy to an exoplanet roughly 153 light years away.

So I did some research for you.

There is only one roughly Earth-mass planet within the habitable zone of its star that is near 153 light years distant. However it has a really lame name. Instead, I propose you pick a gas giant. Yes, I know life would never evolve in the toxic atmosphere of a gas giant, but it very likely could on one of its moons. Seriously, exomoons are a thing, and they're awesome. I recommend choosing HD 133131 A b. It is 153.1 light years distant. It is 2.83 times more massive than Jupiter. Also, the star it orbits is very similar to the Sun, and is part of a triple star system (three stars locked in a gravitational dance in space). Unless I missed something, I think this would fix the plot hole.

Thanks for writing an amazing SCP article!

item.title: Saw this today
item.content:encoded:
https://www.cnet.com/news/jupiter-just-got-slammed-by-something-so-big-we-saw-it-from-earth/
Made me think of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man oh man this is a scary one. Just being that deep in Jupiter's atmosphere, and the sheer pressure and violence involved in Jupiter's climate, is basically the equivalent of continuously being bombarded with kinetic energy far in excess of every explosion that has ever occurred on Earth. And that is just like, what it is experiencing every single day.

I don't think the em pulse is valid as a potential countermeasure, though. It doesn't really detract from the story in any way, to be fair, but I feel like if you're gonna write a story about blocking the communicative EM signals a superadvanced hostile alien device is trying to send to its hyperadvanced hostile alien masters, it might help to know some of the basics of how electromagnetism works. I realize that this shit is long, i really did not intend to write this much, but i kept thinking of more important pieces of info, and it just sorta got out of hand. I dont vouch for the correctness of any of this, this is just sorta what i have picked up from dabbling in hard scifi like greg egan and satisfying my own curiosity

Giant wall of text here:

+ show block
– hide block

It helps to remember that electromagnetic radiation of any kind, from radio waves to gamma radiation to the visible light spectrum to x-rays all the way accross the spectrum… it's all just light. It's all just photons, of varying wavelengths and therefore varying amounts of energy. Of course, if you asked a layman to describe everything they know or can kinda remember about photons, they pretty much only think of the attributes that photons in the visible spectrum have; they know that some photons get absorbed and others get reflected based on their wavelength and/or frequency, and that's what determines the color of a given object, and some photons pass through or don't because of mumble mumble polarity something or other mumble mumble, and that determines the transparency of an object.

But they wouldn't think of also describing how radio works, or how xray machines work, unless you actually like, prompted them with a reminder that there was a whole spectrum of possible photon wavelengths and frequencies outside of the visible. Which sucks, because the discrete, particle-mimicking nature of photons is pretty much only relevant with visible light; when talking about other portions of the spectrum, we pretty much automatically think of it in terms of the amount of energy in a wave, instead of the number of photons each a little discrete packet of "1 energy".

Why is this relevant? Because it gives us a more-intuitive understanding of, say, the sun's magnetosphere. Even people who failed 6th grade science can sorta remember that the sun is shooting out tons of photons. That's why it's bright. It's a big fusion reactor, and (some of) the resultant energy gets emitted as photons that happen to be in the visible range of wavelengths, and we can detect photons of those wavelengths with our retinas. Our brains can then analyze the pattern of reflected vs absorbed photons to deduce information about our surroundings.

We evolved to see specifically the visible portion of the EM spectrum for a reason, as opposed to radio or xrays or whatever. It's because the atmosphere of the earth happens to be fully transparent to visible light, while almost all solid objects happen to be opaque, and this is really useful for navigating our environment. But, and this is the point i am trying to hammer in, there's nothing special about this specific portion. The entire electromagnetic spectrum works exactly the same way: it's just photons of varying wavelength. Because a photon's velocity is fixed at c, the amount of energy carried by a photon is strictly a matter of its wavelength (imagine if higher-pitched soundwaves were always louder than lower-pitched soundwaves, that's pretty much how energy and light works, higher-frequency photons are always more energetic)

next piece of context: a bit on the radio portion of the spectrum. Basically, radio stations are big lightbulbs that create a photon stream way above the visible range, which happens to pass through most solid objects reasonably well, and which is high up enough on the logrythmic scale that there's a lot of 'space' available on the absolute scale for lots of different concurrent channels of communication. You take a photon stream of, say, 101.5 mhz (which on your car radio would be the station 101.5), and then you start twiddling the frequency of the photons in the photon stream up and down, through a range from (hypothetically) 101.47 to 101.53 mhz. And if you plotted the change in frequency over time, it would look like a sound wave. And your radio takes that sound wave and sends it to your car speakers. That's frequency modulation. (There's also amplitude modulation, but I don't understand it, I'm just a dilletante.) With your car radio, it comes out as an analogue soundwave, but you could also just switch between 101.49 and 101.51 mhz to do a binary message. That means you can send arbitrary computer data, too – and indeed, that's how your router works.

Why do radiowaves decay? As the wave moves outward, it still 'flickers' back and forth between peak and valley at the same frequency, but the heights slowly decrease asymptotically back to zero. In theory, you could continue detecting a signal indefinitely far out with sensitive enough gear, as it never actually reaches zero. In practice, however, there are a lot of other EM waves traversing the same space. If your signal is still the most energetic signal in that frequency at the receiver's location, it will be distinguishable from all the other waves, which will appear as noticable complex distortion patterns in the waveform. But eventually on earth, the wave will become weaker than the radio waves put out by the earth's constantly-spinning iron content changing position relative to other iron content.

What about out in space? Well, the biggest source of EM around is the sun. As previously mentioned, it is spewing out an incredible amount of photons, some of which are indeed in the radio range. Sometimes there are disturbances in the sun that emerge as sunspots, flares that shoot out into a ballistic trajectory then fall back to the surface; when such disturbances break the surface and crash back, there are usually enormous EM pulses heavily concentrated in the radio spectrum which have sufficient energy to increase the threshold of energy that a radio transmission must have to be detectable, by increasing the background noise even all the way here at earth. (Just like how the sun's visible light is usually concentrated in the yellow range but sun spots are concentrated in the red range, the sun usually isn't super concentrated in radio, but sun spots are.) That's why sunspots interfere with radio even here on earth, and it's why radio communication's difficulty has been wildly different at times over the past few hundred years.

We are lucky that Earth has an iron core, and is rotating, because it means the EM environment around Earth is dominated by Earth-generated signals. Remember, certain ranges of the EM spectrum can fucking kill you. Just ask anyone at Chernobyl. We are being bombarded by a lot of those signals, but the Earth's magnetosphere is strong enough to basically magnetically overpower those signals. Well, I say signals but since the data content of the EM waves is random noise, caused by the random pertubations of the sun, it's better to say radiation. Others aren't susceptible to being canceled out by stronger EM in the same frequency, but get absorbed by certain substances in our atmosphere like the ozone (the same way a green leaf absorbs everything but green photon waves, leaving them to get reflected to your eyes)

We are used to most photons being pretty safe. Parts of the visible spectrum on up to UV can give you sunburn, but it's not a huge deal. Most of the dangerous ones are really high up in the spectrum, and we either carefully control exposure (like with xray radiation), we simply don't encounter them at all (like with gamma radiation), or the ones we do encounter are nowhere near energetic enough to harm us (like microwave). But absolutely any part of the EM spectrum can kill you dead if it's got enough energy (or, in classical terms, if there are enough photons). Most of the spectrum can permeate air but gets absorbed by solids or liquids. That means it creates heat energy when it hits us. Mostly we think of this as being an attribute of visible light and not others, because the sun gives off visible light and warms us, and you'd never put your hands around a radio to keep them warm. But photons are photons are photons, and heat energy is heat energy.

Alright, now that we have all the background context, let's talk about Jupiter. Most planets dominate their local EM environment; this means that, within the locality of the planet, they have their own magnetosphere that pushes the sun's magnetosphere away. Without this, the solar wind and various cosmic rays and stuff would reach all the way down to the surface, ionize the atmosphere, and make shit really really chaotic (this happens to Venus; Venus's magnetosphere can overpower the sun's only up to about the inner core, so the solar magnetosphere reaches all the way down to the surface). But Jupiter's magnetosphere is so powerful, so dense, so full of static signals of high intensity, that it reaches all the way out beyond all of Jupiter's moons before it is overpowered by the sun. It is incredibly powerful. In order for the Juno space probe to not be literally torn apart by the heat energy from the EM emissions and the various charged particles from the magnetism, it held a polar orbit (EM is focused around the equator, because the rotation of the core is what causes all this stuff to begin with) which dipped down into Jupiter's deep magnetosphere only very briefly, took a few sensor readings and prayed it wouldn't happen to get hit by too many gamma rays or jovian wind, then resurfaced back up where it could get a signal to send the data back.

What does all this mean for SCP-2399?

It means we are totally fucked. Anything that is powerful enough to overpower Jupiter's EM environment and get a radio signal out from inside Jupiter's surface must necessarily have a signal strength commensurately stronger than Jupiter itself. And remember, Jupiter is essentially a radio broadcaster the size of Jupiter, broadcasting with the energy of every sol-originated photon that ever hit Jupiter's enormous surface.

Like, literally, even if we took all of the energy that has ever existed on Earth, every photon ever captured by every plant, every bit of heat that ever hit a speck of dirt, and shoved all that energy at a radio broadcaster positioned exactly between Jupiter and the alien galaxy, we still wouldn't be within a thousandth of a percent of overpowering Jupiter's EM emissions themselves, much less something that could overpower Jupiter from inside Jupiter's EM environment.

So how the hell is an EMP supposed to fry something that exists inside the largest permanently-pulsing EMP in our solar system? By managing to function inside Jupiter, it has already demonstrated that it is so well shielded as to be immune to any electromagnetic interference our puny little blue planet could ever dish out. An EMP powerful enough to destroy SCP-2399 would probably need enough energy to scour the entire solar system clean of all of its atomic bonds (like what happens when you aim Gamma EM radiation at biological molecules, only several orders of magnitude worse)

The bottom line is, the kinetic energy from hanging out in Jupiter's high-pressure climate for just a few seconds is already many million times higher than all of the bombs detonated and bullets fired on earth, and the EM radiation from hanging out beneath the surface of Jupiter for just a few seconds is already many million times higher than all of the EM ever generated on the planet earth. Some simple Fermi calculations show that even if you launched the entire mass of planet Earth as a ballistic projectile directly at SCP-2399, it wouldn't even be noticable compared to the violence already being inflicted. Remember, the entire planet Earth could fit into just the atmosphere of Jupiter's red spot about 15 times by volume, and about 240 times by mass (jupiter is really dense lol)

Any actual solution to this problem is going to involve using some kind of anomalous means to simply break all of the limits. Like, if we used the infinite potato sack as a fuel source until we had a supply of energy sufficient to just overpower jupiter, or if we got the immovable object and the unstoppable force to collide on top of SCP-2399, or something like that. The problem is, any solution like that would destroy the Earth at the same time that it destroyed Jupiter. There would be no way to keep Earth from getting caught up in the splash zone.

Alright, i'm done, sorry for enormous rant. Again, i like this SCP just fine the way it is, I just think it could be a little better if the specific scientific reasons for helpless despair were a little more accurate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cue 682 returning to Earth piloting a planet-busting, god-killing spaceship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like this SCP, very scary and very well written. And I have the perfect solution to deal with it!

Throw 682 at it. One/both of them will be destroyed or permanently distracted in the process.
But seriously, good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anyone noticing the connection to scp-179? scp-179 is said to been pointing one of it's arms toward [DATA EXPUNGED], which would 100% make sense to be scp-2399, given that scp-179 detects extraterrestial threats, and have been pointing towards [DATA EXPUNGED] since some time in the 1900s

Did perhaps the author mean this connection?

item.title: random thought....
item.content:encoded:
if the foundation knows how to decrypt the messages intended for 2399, why not just encrypt a message and tell 2399 to stop repairing itself and fly off elsewhere?

Edit: apparently ooblex already brought this up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has the Solidarity ever fought this thing in a tale? I imagine 2399 would probably have the upper hand if it were fully repaired, but I bet the Grand Wave Motion Cannon could do some damage.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: That is a good suggestion, I'll be adding that as well.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1396 is a pretty obscure SCP which seems pretty pertinent. A good way to include it without altering the flow of the SCP's story would be something like:

Efforts to develop alternative methods of eliminating SCP-2399 are currently underway. {Footnote} The repair of SCP-1396 has been proposed, but has been deemed currently unfeasible. {/Footnote}

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

First crosslink I am proposing is to a tale about the fallout and the ramifications of Protocol LEGIONNAIRE, which is The Last Man. This is a tale about 2399, and therefore can be linked to. Changing:

So we devised Protocol LEGIONNAIRE. One gigantic EMP, powered by god-knows-what, followed by a volley of nukes big enough to wipe out our civilization a thousand times over.

To:

So we devised Protocol LEGIONNAIRE. One gigantic EMP, powered by god-knows-what, followed by a volley of nukes big enough to wipe out our civilization a thousand times over.

The second crosslink I am proposing is to one of the author's 001 proposals, in which 2399 makes an appearance. This is a large SCP with many crosslinks itself, but 2399 plays a major role in the conclusion. Editing:

██/██/2015- Unit is out of range of target: Proceed to planet #3 in system [COORDINATES REDACTED]: Priority is target: Cease repairs

To:

██/██/2015- Unit is out of range of target: Proceed to planet #3 in system [COORDINATES REDACTED]: Priority is target: Cease repairs

If anyone has any other idea, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, Some notes:

The Red Spot is shrinking. The system's biggest storm is dying down. What does that mean for SCP-2399?

Last night, as I gazed through my looking glass, I saw what appeared to be a star of great luminescence streak through the far reaches of our solar system. I have never recorded an object moving so fast; it had surpassed the outer planets in fewer than two hours! As I watched, by my own two eyes, I saw it slow as it closed on Jupiter,

If the object was traveling towards Earth, it wouldn't appear to moving at all to an observer on Earth, unless its impact was months out and it was targeting Earth plus some distance. Even if he was in a position to see the object's velocity fully expressed, the distance (about 30 light-minutes at closest approach) would make any object below c appear to be slower than a shooting star. It's the same reason as why planes seem to hang in the air, while cars zip by.

I swear on my life that I observed what looked to me like explosions and starbursts eminating from our red spot. I fear my mind is playing tricks on me, for there has been no record of such violent outbursts by a heavenly body since the dawn of astronomy.

European and Chinese records match for an explosion on the moon in the 12th century. I can't say with certainty that Cassini was aware of the European record, but it seems likely. Also, emAnating is misspelled, unless this is a British-ism I'm unfamiliar with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
silently having A panic Attack
Awesome!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I say this as no offense to djkaktus, as they are one of my favourite authors on this site, and 2399 is a personal favoruite of mine—not in a scary sense, but more of, “Oh, there is implications of an interesting story here”.

This video just found and delivered that story int eh best possible way, and was able to retroactively make 2399 not something I look at and go, “Oh, that’s cool”, but look at with a similar abject horror once experiences at the mention of a Lovecraftian Deity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, time to give some praise to our Russian friends in the SCP Community, because I just found some pretty good fanart (I believe it’s made by the person who was working on that SCP Artbook a while back; can’t fund a finer source, since the picture is only on Russian booru equivalents, as far as I’ve been able to find) of 2399.

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: Ehhhhhhch
item.content:encoded:
I’m going to neutral vote this for one big reason.
The blackboxing. More specifically the black boxing in the later addendums, where at one point over half the addendum is black boxed. Now, I’m all for blackboxing our things we don’t need to know or confidential, but this is just kinda too much for me. It really just kinda ruined the whole article for me, sorry to say.

item.title: Easy Solution?
item.content:encoded:
Why doesn't the foundation just order it to go away or destroy itself? The messages were decrypted, so surely they could encrypt their own orders and send it to the skip.

And I know the article said they don't know "how it receives communications" (not a direct quote), but I interpreted that as meaning receiving communications from another galaxy, so far away, and at such specific timing.

Thoughts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the conclusion there sirpudding. I almost though it was an unintentional reference to Giygas from Earthbound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lmao alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Gigas" is the singular form of "Gigantes", the race of Giants in Greek myth and the word from which we get "giant" from. Two unrelated things with the same Greek mythic reference is an "epic fail"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or, it's an intentional reference because both of them present themselves as red, swirling chaotic masses that have the potential to end the world as we know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Project Gigas?

Little did the writters of this article know there is a final boss in a Nintendo game named "Giygas".

I dub that as an epic fail XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a contender for my all time favorite SCP. As an avid science fiction fanatic, a planet-busting alien superweapon located on Jupiter just tingles all of my Sci-Fi nerves. Amazing work!

item.title: Aliens on Jupiter?
item.content:encoded:
Nice, never really thought about it.
Creative, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, electromagnetic shielding is the most realistic form of shielding. It's what's keeping you and I from being vaporized by radiation and what not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was actually a very enjoyable read. Even if it was a bit lengthy and a little hard to believe, still a bit spine chilling. If it eats moons, then how in the world are all those nukes going to get through, though? That's just a bit of a "It's invincible! Let's throw tiny rocks at it to see how invincible it is!" beef that makes the Foundation slightly… unintelligible.

+ Spoilers
- Hi there

Still a good SCP, entertaining to say the least… but I want to know how it got to Earth and then went to Jupiter after the SCP-001 offer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thinking about it, if things go south, this might be the only SCP other than 2317 that you could feasibly label as Apollyon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know…

I want to like this, but there's some clinical tone issues in the description that just break the immersion for me. And past that point, the addendums following don't really make up for it either. I think it's trying to invoke a sense of dread in the final part, but kinda falls flat by the time I get there. Nothing glaringly wrong with it, just does nothing for me. Sorry.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry, Commander Shepard will take care of SCP-2399 and all other Reapers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word "gigas" has been around for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like crossovers here.

And I can assure you, it isn't. They might have thought it could be an "Original name" without even knowing about EarthBound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a minor suggestion, but there would be no need to assault the SCP with nuclear warheads and specialized EMP devices. Nuclear weapons emit their own powerful EMP as it is.

Unless, of course, for some reason the idea is that an initial EMP strike would be designed to degrade electronic capabilities before piling on with nuclear blast effects, but perhaps that ought to be specified…

/end nitpicking

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TBH, that video is scarier than the skip. I want the guy who wrote that script on this site, writing alongside us, because he (or she) is absolutely great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I keep coming back to this article (this time from Sunny's art page, where I had never seen her art for this one before) and I just wanted to say that it will probably always be one of my favorites. The scale, the direness of the situation, the graphics, it all works so well.

I'd love to know kaktus' opinion of the movie linked by Akasen up above—I thought it was incredibly well done.

item.title: Infame Kato
item.content:encoded:
I LOVE that channel and I was about to make the same comment myself.

I've been showing that video to people and urging them to like, comment, and subscribe. I want the creator to get encouragement and support, because they sure as hell deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Computer models estimate that SCP-2399 is at 59% completion, with a current rate of .78% annually. This rate has increased from an estimated .12% in 1970.

Upvoted there

The second-to-last addendum really gets me. Just the idea of the Foundation throwing everything and more at this thing delights me. Awesome work.

item.title: Other Jovian SCPs
item.content:encoded: If you really want to stitch this into the SCP Universe, mention that contact was attempted with SCP-1396 to assist in destroying it. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am here to share this little youtube video because this is how I discovered this SCP.

I think I will read over this a couple more times throughout the week. Space is already pretty terrifying. This SCP does not help that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cause the 100th up vote. You're-fucking-welcome

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WE SAW THE SOUND AND HEARD THE LIGHT

Is this "standard" moonhowl spice or does this particular log getting it is plot-relevant?

My only real complaint is that "EMP, then -hypernuke it" seems like a rather unimaginative method of attempted dispatch, in-universe. I know some people prefer a low sci-fi Foundationverse, but I still feel true pragmatism is about the clever use of your resources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda late, but I just saw this.

Even thought this thing is a threat, there are way more dangerous SCPs that are a much bigger threat than this. So wasting every resource on this seems stupid since the Foundation has other threats it has to keep at bay at the moment. So if they truly use every resource they have at their disposal, not only will they have nothing to stop existing Keters, but also they won't be able to continue containment of Euclids or containing new found SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, they're both Cassini. You're seeing this:

Giovanni Cassini

And thinking Galileo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So was it Galileo that saw 2399 hit Io and get trapped by Jupiter or Cassini? The diary is Galileo, but Addendum 2399-L5 says Cassini.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To protect those who can't protect themselves from things that they wouldn't even known to protect themselves from.

Caught that nearly a year later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I once read a very bad sci fi novel ("Calculating God" if anyone wants to suffer it) where they posited the explanation for there being so few sapient races or evidence of such in the galaxy was due to two things:

1 - The time between discovering radio and the singularity where everyone uploads into an underground computer matrix to live forever as virtual gods is pretty short on the cosmic scale.

2 - All the races that have done so in the past try to preserve their immortality by obliterating every species in the galaxy that might someday become advanced enough to visit their now unpopulated worlds to mess with their hard drives.

The second premise is absolutely ridiculous but because the main character theorized it it turned out to be true (this was a constant event in a very bad book).

Anyway I am not mentioning that to complain. The concept of other planets going to silly lengths to murder us was dumb in that book. In the Foundation Universe, I actually like it. Someone could be trying to contain or neutralize us. Or, as hinted above, we just made someone WANT to by being a nuisance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given what my headcanon actually is about this article, that last bit is a pretty good insight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it created the Giant Red Spot on Jupiter. That thing is the size of three Earths. It poses a threat, and if they wait until it is repaired, it'll likely be too late to do anything to stop it. It's kinda like saying "The dam protecting the metropolitian area will burst in about 25 years if we don't fix it and we can't fix it just yet, but we have other things to do right now, so we won't plan fixing the dam until it bursts."

… Granted, I think the Foundation sorta caused this thing to want to destroy Earth due to the containment efforts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it quite a bit until the third addendum. From there on it just lost me.

a platform of ██ warheads bearing ███ Mt payloads and ██ warheads bearing EMP detonators was launched and placed in orbit around Europa.

But we must try. We must do something. Even if we have to drain our banks and empty our mines, we must try.

This seems extremely excessive, mostly because it isn't much of a threat in its current state. Why would the Foundation use all of its resources to destroy something that isn't any danger to people (at least not yet) while they have much worse things to deal with down on Earth?

I'm not gonna downvote, seeing as I was pretty hooked during the first half of the article, but I'm not going to upvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if we manage to delay the machine until the Crab Nebula comes?

And what would happen if we launched SCP 2845 at it? I mean, its on Jupiter, so there can't be too much fall out, and we can just hide the observatory results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this one, although I do agree with some others in that we shouldn't have too many SCPs that are totally going to destroy the Earth with any hope of saving us left to the future. Fast-forward 30 years and we've been fucked 5 times over already, although after reading a few foundation tales I suppose it has happened before.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this one quite a bit. Really like the last bits with the Addendums, seeing the layers pulled back with each one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a 7 MB animated GIF whose native resolution is twice that of the actual image box. That's about twenty times the size it ought to be to be optimized for the web.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this article, but the first image lapse thing is really lagging my computer. Is there any alternative you could use? Nevermind, works now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this one to be alright. A tad cliché, but the execution was pretty good. +1.

item.title: Re: Needs Grammar Work
item.content:encoded: Ah. I apologize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If they succeeded, who would be left to write an article about it?

Not me, that's for sure.
